description: MetLife is a global financial services company centered on insurance, employee benefits, annuities, retirement income products, and institutional asset management.

overview.title: Business Profile
paragraphs: MetLife is best understood as a large-scale risk pooling and benefits infrastructure company: it sells protection, retirement, and income products to individuals, employers, and institutions, then manages the financial machinery required to keep those promises credible.

Its core offerings include life insurance, dental and vision coverage, disability coverage, accident and health products, annuities, pension risk transfer solutions, and investment management services. The branding is reassuring by design; the business model is actuarial discipline with a very large customer-service wrapper.

overview.title: Employee Benefits Footprint
paragraphs: MetLife has a major presence in U.S. group benefits, serving employers and brokers with life, disability, dental, vision, accident, critical illness, hospital indemnity, legal plans, pet insurance, and other workplace-linked products.

This makes the company relevant to Free The World not just as an insurer, but as part of the benefits administration layer that shapes how millions of workers encounter financial security, healthcare-adjacent coverage, and payroll-deducted protection products.

overview.title: Life Insurance And Retirement
paragraphs: Life insurance remains central to MetLife's public identity and product base, although the company also operates across retirement and income solutions such as institutional annuities, structured settlements, pension risk transfers, and post-retirement benefit funding.

These products sit in a conservative corner of financial technology: the user-facing surface may be forms, calculators, and portals, but the strategic moat is pricing risk, managing reserves, distribution relationships, and regulatory capital.

overview.title: Asset Management
paragraphs: MetLife Investment Management extends the company beyond traditional insurance underwriting into institutional asset management, where it manages public fixed income, private capital, real estate, and other investment strategies.

The asset-management arm matters because insurers do not merely collect premiums and wait patiently; they invest large pools of capital, and the performance and risk profile of those portfolios help determine pricing power, earnings volatility, and long-term claims-paying capacity.

overview.title: Registry Relevance
paragraphs: For the registry, MetLife should initially be mapped around life insurance, employee benefits, retirement income, pension risk transfer, and institutional asset management rather than consumer fintech alone.

Open or decentralized alternatives may be thinner here than in software markets, because insurance depends on regulated balance sheets and claims administration. Still, comparison targets can include open benefits administration tooling, mutual-aid risk pools, cooperative insurance models, portable benefits infrastructure, open payroll integrations, and transparent retirement-planning tools.
